content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Every product team has one relationship that is both fascinating and frustrating at the same time: the relationship between Product Managers and Designers.
It’s almost like watching two people trying to reach the same destination while using completely different maps.
A Product Manager is constantly asking, “Will this help the product grow? Will users buy it? Will it improve conversion? Will it increase engagement?”
A Designer, on the other hand, keeps asking, “Will users understand this? Is this intuitive? Does this solve a real problem? Can someone complete this task without confusion?”
Neither perspective is wrong.
In fact, both are absolutely necessary.
But that’s exactly why the clash never disappears.

Throughout my career, I’ve noticed that this tension exists in almost every project. PMs naturally think from the business perspective. Designers naturally think from the user’s perspective. Somewhere in the middle lies a great product.
The challenge begins when one perspective completely overshadows the other.
One incident still makes me smile.
I was asked to create a wireframe for an e-commerce project. Like every UX designer would, I didn’t immediately jump into beautiful screens. I spent time understanding the user journey, identifying usability issues, studying competitors, and fixing interaction problems before opening Figma.
After a few hours of work, I proudly presented the wireframe to my Product Manager.
He looked at it for a few seconds.
Then came the feedback.
“It just looks like boxes and rectangles.”
I paused for a second.
In my head, I wanted to say,
“Well… that’s exactly what a wireframe is.”
A wireframe isn’t supposed to be visually attractive. It isn’t meant to impress stakeholders with gradients, shadows, glassmorphism, or fancy illustrations. Its only purpose is to answer one question:
Does the experience make sense?
Boxes represent content.
Rectangles represent images.
Lines represent text.
It’s intentionally boring because it removes visual distractions and allows everyone to focus on structure and user flow.
But sometimes, expectations are different.
And that’s okay.
Or at least, I thought so.

As the project progressed, something interesting kept happening.
Almost every design I created was rejected.
Not because of usability.
Not because the user journey was broken.
Not because accessibility was ignored.
The feedback was usually something along the lines of:
“This isn’t what I expected.”
Fair enough.
So I did what I believe every designer should do.
I asked a simple question.
“Could you help me understand your expectations?”
Because if expectations aren’t clear, no amount of iterations will magically arrive at the right solution.
Instead of an explanation, I received a document.
Not a product requirement document.
Not user stories.
Not acceptance criteria.
A Claude-generated document.
At first, I thought it would explain the business goals.
Instead, it described the design itself.
Where the cards should be placed.
How the layout should look.
Which sections should appear first.
What the screen should resemble.
For a moment, I wasn’t sure whether I was reading product requirements or design specifications.
And that made me realize something.
Sometimes, Product Managers unknowingly become Designers.
Not because they want to.
But because modern AI tools make it incredibly easy to generate UI ideas.
The problem is that generating a layout isn’t the same as designing an experience.

AI has changed the way we work.
Today, anyone can generate a beautiful screen in seconds.
Prompt in.
Design out.
It feels magical.
But product design has never been about drawing screens.
It’s about understanding people.
Research.
Behavior.
Context.
Mental models.
Trade-offs.
Accessibility.
Business constraints.
Edge cases.
These are things AI can assist with, but not replace.
When a generated layout becomes the requirement itself, we risk designing for screenshots instead of users.

I also realized something about designers.
We aren’t perfect either.
Sometimes we become too attached to our research.
Too protective of our design systems.
Too eager to defend every spacing value and every interaction pattern.
Not every business decision is anti-user.
Not every PM suggestion is wrong.
Sometimes there are market realities, technical constraints, timelines, revenue targets, and strategic decisions that designers don’t fully see.
That’s why collaboration matters more than ownership.

The best Product Managers I’ve worked with never tried to design the interface.
They explained the business problem.
They shared customer insights.
They defined success metrics.
Then they trusted designers to solve the experience.
Similarly, the best designers I’ve worked with didn’t treat design as art.
They understood revenue.
They cared about retention.
They asked about business goals.
They measured outcomes.
That’s where great products are born.
Not when PMs become designers.
Not when designers ignore business.
But when both respect each other’s expertise.

Looking back, I don’t remember the rejected wireframes anymore.
I don’t even remember the final screens.
What I remember is the lesson.
Communication is more valuable than iterations.
Clear expectations are more valuable than endless feedback.
And trust between Product Managers and Designers is far more valuable than any AI-generated document.
At the end of the day, users don’t care who won the argument.
They only care whether the product solves their problem.
And maybe that’s the one thing both PMs and Designers have been trying to achieve all along.
